title: Word Frequency Counter
description:
Read a single line of text. For each unique word (compared case-insensitively), print `word count` on its own line.

Lines should be sorted **alphabetically** by word, and all words should be printed in lowercase.

conceptLesson.title: Counting with Dictionaries
conceptLesson.content: A dict makes counting easy: use `counts.get(key, 0) + 1` to increment, or import `Counter` from `collections`. `sorted(d)` returns the keys in sorted order.

hints: Lowercase the input first so case is ignored.
Loop over the words; use `counts.get(w, 0) + 1` to update.
Iterate over `sorted(counts)` to print in alphabetical order.

examples.input: the quick brown fox the lazy dog
examples.output:
brown 1
dog 1
fox 1
lazy 1
quick 1
the 2

testCases.input: the quick brown fox the lazy dog
testCases.expectedOutput:
brown 1
dog 1
fox 1
lazy 1
quick 1
the 2

starterCode: # Read a line and print 'word count' for each unique word, sorted alphabetically

title: Merge Dictionaries
description:
Read two dictionaries on two lines. Each dictionary is a list of `key:value` pairs separated by commas, e.g. `a:1,b:2`. Values are integers.

Merge them: if a key appears in both, **sum** the values. Print every key in the merged dict on its own line as `key:value`, sorted alphabetically by key.

An empty line represents an empty dictionary.

conceptLesson.title: Merging Dictionaries
conceptLesson.content: To merge two dicts and combine overlapping keys, start from one dict and update from the other. Use `merged.get(k, 0) + v` to safely add values whether or not the key already exists.

hints: Write a small parser that splits on `,` and then `:`.
Handle the case where a line is empty — return an empty dict.
When merging, use `merged.get(k, 0) + v` so missing keys default to 0.
starterCode: # Parse two key:value lines, merge by summing on duplicate keys, print sorted
